book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
On the Words, Maker of Heaven and Earth, and of All Things Visible and Invisible.

ccel.title: NPNF2-07. Cyril of Jerusalem, Gregory Nazianzen

source.title: NPNF2-07. Cyril of Jerusalem, Gregory Nazianzen

title: On the Words, Maker of Heaven and Earth, and of All Things Visible and Invisible.

source.title: NPNF2-07. Cyril of Jerusalem, Gregory Nazianzen

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who is this that hideth counsel from Me, and keepeth words in his heart, and thinketh to hide them from Me?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: To look upon God with eyes of flesh is impossible: for the incorporeal cannot be subject to bodily sight: and the Only begotten Son of God Himself hath testified, saying, No man hath seen God at any time.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For if according to that which is written in Ezekiel any one should understand that Ezekiel saw Him, yet what saith the Scripture?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He saw the likeness of the glory of the Lord; not the Lord Himself, but the likeness of His glory, not the glory itself, as it really is.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And when he saw merely the likeness of the glory, and not the glory itself, he fell to the earth from fear.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Now if the sight of the likeness of the glory brought fear and distress upon the prophets, any one who should attempt to behold God Himself would to a certainty lose his life, according to the saying, No man shall see My face and live.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For this cause God of His great loving-kindness spread out the heaven as a veil of His proper Godhead, that we should not perish.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The word is not mine, but the Prophet’s.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If Thou shalt rend the heavens, trembling will take hold of the mountains at sight of Thee, and they will flow down.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And why dost thou wonder that Ezekiel fell down on seeing the likeness of the glory? when Daniel at the sight of Gabriel, though but a servant of God, straightway shuddered and fell on his face, and, prophet as he was, dared not answer him, until the Angel transformed himself into the likeness of a son of man.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Now if the appearing of Gabriel wrought trembling in the Prophets, had God Himself been seen as He is, would not all have perished?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The Divine Nature then it is impossible to see with eyes of flesh: but from the works, which are Divine, it is possible to attain to some conception of His power, according to Solomon, who says, For by the greatness and beauty of the creatures proportionably the Maker of them is seen.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He said not that from the creatures the Maker is seen, but added proportionably.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For God appears the greater to every man in proportion as he has grasped a larger survey of the creatures: and when his heart is uplifted by that larger survey, he gains withal a greater conception of God.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Wouldest thou learn that to comprehend the nature of God is impossible?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The Three Children in the furnace of fire, as they hymn the praises of God, say Blessed art thou that beholdest the depths, and sittest upon the Cherubim.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Tell me what is the nature of the Cherubim, and then look upon Him who sitteth upon them.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And yet Ezekiel the Prophet even made a description of them, as far as was possible, saying that every one has four faces, one of a man, another of a lion, another of an eagle, and another of a calf; and that each one had six wings, and they had eyes on all sides; and that under each one was a wheel of four sides.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Nevertheless though the Prophet makes the explanation, we cannot yet understand it even as we read.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But if we cannot understand the throne, which he has described, how shall we be able to comprehend Him who sitteth thereon, the Invisible and Ineffable God?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: To scrutinise then the nature of God is impossible: but it is in our power to send up praises of His glory for His works that are seen.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: These things I say to you because of the following context of the Creed, and because we say, We Believe in One God, the Father Almighty, Maker of Heaven and Earth, and of All Things Visible and Invisible; in order that we may remember that the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ is the same as He that made the heaven and the earth, and that we may make ourselves safe against the wrong paths of the godless heretics, who have dared to speak evil of the All wise Artificer of all this world, men who see with eyes of flesh, but have the eyes of their understanding blinded.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For what fault have they to find with the vast creation of God?—they, who ought to have been struck with amazement on beholding the vaultings of the heavens: they, who ought to have worshipped Him who reared the sky as a dome, who out of the fluid nature of the waters formed the stable substance of the heaven.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For God said, Let there be a firmament in the midst of the water.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: God spake once for all, and it stands fast, and falls not.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The heaven is water, and the orbs therein, sun, moon, and stars are of fire: and how do the orbs of fire run their course in the water?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But if any one disputes this because of the opposite natures of fire and water, let him remember the fire which in the time of Moses in Egypt flamed amid the hail, and observe the all-wise workmanship of God.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For since there was need of water, because the earth was to be tilled, He made the heaven above of water that when the region of the earth should need watering by showers, the heaven might from its nature be ready for this purpose.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Is there not cause to wonder when one looks at the constitution of the sun?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For being to the sight as it were a small body he contains a mighty power; appearing from the East, and sending forth his light unto the West: whose rising at dawn the Psalmist described, saying: And he cometh forth out of his chamber as a bridegroom.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: He was describing the brightness and moderation of his state on first becoming visible unto men: for when he rides at high noon, we often flee from his blaze: but at his rising he is welcome to all as a bridegroom to look on.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Observe also his arrangement (or rather not his, but the arrangement of Him who by an ordinance determined his course), how in summer he rises higher and makes the days longer, giving men good time for their works: but in winter contracts his course, that the period of cold may be increased, and that the nights becoming longer may contribute to men’s rest, and contribute also to the fruitfulness of the products of the earth.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: See also how the days alternately respond each to other in due order, in summer increasing, and in winter diminishing; but in spring and autumn granting equal intervals one to another.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And the nights again complete the like courses; so that the Psalmist also says of them, Day unto day uttereth speech, and night unto night proclaimeth knowledge.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For to the heretics who have no ears, they all but cry aloud, and by their good order say, that there is none other God save the Creator who hath set them their bounds, and laid out the order of the Universe.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But let no one tolerate any who say that one is the Creator of the light, and another of darkness: for let him remember how Isaiah says, I am the God who made the light, and created darkness.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Why, O man, art thou vexed thereat?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Why art thou offended at the time that is given thee for rest?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: A servant would have had no rest from his masters, had not the darkness necessarily brought a respite.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And often after wearying ourselves in the day, how are we refreshed in the night, and he who was yesterday worn with toils, rises vigorous in the morning because of the night’s rest?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And what more helpful to wisdom than the night?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For herein oftentimes we set before our minds the things of God; and herein we read and contemplate the Divine Oracles.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And when is our mind most attuned to Psalmody and Prayer?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Is it not at night?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And when have we often called our own sins to remembrance?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Is not at night?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Let us not then admit the evil thought, that another is the maker of darkness: for experience shews that this also is good and useful.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: They ought to have felt astonishment and admiration not only at the arrangement of sun and moon, but also at the well-ordered choirs of the stars, their unimpeded courses, and their risings in the seasons due to each: and how some are signs of summer, and others of winter; and how some mark the season for sowing, and others shew the commencement of navigation.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And a man sitting in his ship, and sailing amid the boundless waves, steers his ship by looking at the stars.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For of these matters the Scripture says well, And let them be for signs, and for seasons, and for years, not for fables of astrology and nativities.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But observe how He has also graciously given us the light of day by gradual increase: for we do not see the sun at once arise; but just a little light runs on before, in order that the pupil of the eye may be enabled by previous trial to look upon his stronger beam: see also how He has relieved the darkness of the night by rays of moonlight.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who is the father of the rain?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And who hath begotten the drops of dew?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who condensed the air into clouds, and bade them carry the waters of the rain, now bringing golden-tinted clouds from the north, now changing these into one uniform appearance, and again transforming them into manifold circles and other shapes?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who can number the clouds in wisdom?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Whereof in Job it saith, And He knoweth the separations of the clouds, and hath bent down the heaven to the earth: and, He who numbereth the clouds in wisdom: and, the cloud is not rent under Him.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For so many measures of waters lie upon the clouds, yet they are not rent: but come down with all good order upon the earth.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who bringeth the winds out of their treasuries?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And who, as we said before, is he that hath begotten the drops of dew?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And out of whose womb cometh the ice?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For its substance is like water, and its strength like stone.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And at one time the water becomes snow like wool, at another it ministers to Him who scattereth the mist like ashes, and at another it is changed into a stony substance; since He governs the waters as He will.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Its nature is uniform, and its action manifold in force.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Water becomes in vines wine that maketh glad the heart of man: and in olives oil that maketh man’s face to shine: and is transformed also into bread that strengtheneth man’s heart, and into fruits of all kinds which He hath created.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: What should have been the effect of these wonders?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Should the Creator have been blasphemed?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Or worshipped rather?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And so far I have said noticing of the unseen works of His wisdom.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Observe, I pray you, the spring, and the flowers of every kind in all their likeness still diverse one from another; the deepest crimson of the rose, and the purest whiteness of the lily: for these spring from the same rain and the same earth, and who makes them to differ?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who fashions them?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Observe, pray, the exact care: from the one substance of the tree there is part for shelter, and part for divers fruits: and the Artificer is One.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Of the same vine part is for burning, and part for shoots, and part for leaves, and part for tendrils, and part for clusters.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Admire also the great thickness of the knots which run round the reed, as the Artificer hath made them.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: From one and the same earth come forth creeping things, and wild beasts, and cattle, and trees, and food; and gold, and silver, and brass, and iron, and stone.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The nature of the waters is but one, yet from it comes the substance of fishes and of birds; whereby as the former swim in the waters, so the birds fly in the air.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: This great and wide sea, therein are things creeping innumerable.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who can describe the beauty of the fishes that are therein?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who can describe the greatness of the whales, and the nature of its amphibious animals, how they live both on dry land and in the waters?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who can tell the depth and the breadth of the sea, or the force of its enormous waves?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Yet it stays at its bounds, because of Him who said, Hitherto shalt thou come, and no further, but within thyself shall thy waves be broken.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Which sea also clearly shews the word of the command imposed upon it, since after it has run up, it leaves upon the beach a visible line made by the waves, shewing, as it were, to those who see it, that it has not passed its appointed bounds.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who can discern the nature of the birds of the air?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: How some carry with them a voice of melody, and others are variegated with all manner of painting on their wings, and others fly up into mid air and float motionless, as the hawk: for by the Divine command the hawk spreadeth out his wings and floateth motionless, looking towards the south.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: What man can behold the eagle’s lofty flight?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If then thou canst not discern the soaring of the most senseless of the birds, how wouldest thou understand the Maker of all?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who among men knows even the names of all wild beasts?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Or who can accurately discern the physiology of each?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But if of the wild beasts we know not even the mere names, how shall we comprehend the Maker of them?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: God’s command was but one, which said, Let the earth bring forth wild beasts, and cattle, and creeping things, after their kinds and from one earth, by one command, have sprung diverse natures, the gentle sheep and the carnivorous lion, and various instincts of irrational animals, bearing resemblance to the various characters of men; the fox to manifest the craft that is in men, and the snake the venomous treachery of friends, and the neighing horse the wantonness of young men, and the laborious ant, to arouse the sluggish and the dull: for when a man passes his youth in idleness, then he is instructed by the irrational animals, being reproved by the divine Scripture saying, Go to the ant, thou sluggard, see and emulate her ways, and become wiser than she.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For when thou seest her treasuring up her food in good season, imitate her, and treasure up for thyself fruits of good works for the world to come.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: And again, Go to the bee, and learn how industrious she is: how, hovering round all kinds of flowers, she collects her honey for thy benefit: that thou also, by ranging over the Holy Scriptures, mayest lay hold of salvation for thyself, and being filled with them mayest say, How sweet are thy words unto my throat, yea sweeter than honey and the honeycomb unto my mouth.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Is not then the Artificer worthy the rather to be glorified?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If thou knowest not the nature of all things, do the things that have been made forthwith become useless?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Canst thou know the efficacy of all herbs?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Or canst thou learn all the benefit which proceeds from every animal?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Ere now even from venomous adders have come antidotes for the preservation of men.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But thou wilt say to me, “The snake is terrible.”

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Fear thou the Lord, and it shall not be able to hurt thee.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: “A scorpion stings.”

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Fear the Lord, and it shall not sting thee.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: “A lion is bloodthirsty.”

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Fear thou the Lord, and he shall lie down beside thee, as by Daniel.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But truly wonderful also is the action of the animals: how some, as the scorpion, have the sharpness in a sting; and others have their power in their teeth; and others do battle with their claws; while the basilisk’s power is his gaze.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: So then from this varied workmanship understand the Creator’s power.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: But these things perhaps thou knowest not: thou wouldest have nothing in common with the creatures which are without thee.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Enter now into thyself, and from thine own nature consider its Artificer.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: What is there to find fault with in the framing of thy body?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Be master of thyself, and nothing evil shall proceed from any of thy members.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Adam was at first without clothing in Paradise with Eve, but it was not because of his members that he deserved to be cast out.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: The members then are not the cause of sin, but they who use their members amiss; and the Maker thereof is wise.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who prepared the recesses of the womb for child-bearing?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who gave life to the lifeless thing within it?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who knitted us with sinews and bones, and clothed us with skin and flesh, and, as soon as the child was born, brought streams of milk out of the breasts?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: How grows the babe into a boy, and the boy into a youth, and then into a man; and, still the same, passes again into an old man, while no one notices the exact change from day to day?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Of the food, how is one part changed into blood, and another separated for excretion, and another part changed into flesh?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who gives to the heart its unceasing motion?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who wisely guarded the tenderness of the eyes with the fence of the eyelids?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For as to the complicated and wonderful contrivance of the eyes, the voluminous books of the physicians hardly give us explanation.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who distributes the one breath to the whole body?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Thou seest, O man, the Artificer, thou seest the wise Creator.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: These points my discourse has now treated at large, having left out many, yea, ten thousand other things, and especially things incorporeal and invisible, that thou mayest abhor those who blaspheme the wise and good Artificer, and from what is spoken and read, and whatever thou canst thyself discover or conceive, from the greatness and beauty of the creatures mayest proportionably see the maker of them, and bending the knee with godly reverence to the Maker of the worlds, the worlds, I mean, of sense and thought, both visible and invisible, thou mayest with a grateful and holy tongue, with unwearied lips and heart, praise God and say, How wonderful are Thy works, O Lord; in wisdom hast Thou made them all.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For to Thee belongeth honour, and glory, and majesty, both now and throughout all ages.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Appendix to Lecture IX.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Note.—In the manuscripts which contain this discourse under the name of “A Homily of S.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Basil on God as Incomprehensible,” some portions are changed to suit that subject: but the conclusion especially is marked by great addition and variation, which it is well to reproduce here.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Accordingly in place of the words in §15: τί μεμπτόν, “What is there to find fault with?” and the following, the manuscripts before mentioned have it thus:

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: “What is there to find fault with in the framing of the body?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Come forth into the midst and speak.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Control thine own will, and nothing evil shall proceed from any of thy members.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: For every one of these has of necessity been made for our use.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Chasten thy reasoning unto piety, submit to God’s commandments, and none of these members sin in working and serving in the uses for which they were made.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: If thou be not willing, the eye sees not amiss, the ear hears nothing which it ought not, the hand is not stretched out for wicked greed, the foot walketh not towards injustice, thou hast no strange loves, committest no fornication, covetest not thy neighbour’s wife.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Drive out wicked thoughts from thine heart, be as God made thee, and thou wilt rather give thanks to thy Creator.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Adam at first was without clothing, faring daintily in Paradise: and after he had received the commandment, but failed to keep it, and wickedly stretched forth his hand (not because the hand wished this, but because his will stretched forth his hand to that which was forbidden), because of his disobedience he lost also the good things he had received.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Thus the members are not the cause of sin to those who use them, but the wicked mind, as the Lord says, For out of the heart proceed evil thoughts, fornications, adulteries, envyings, and such like.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: In what things thou choosest, therein thy limbs serve thee; they are excellently made for the service of the soul: they are provided as servants to thy reason.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Guide them well by the motion of piety; bridle them by the fear of God; bring them into subjection to the desire of temperance and abstinence, and they will never rise up against thee to tyrannise over thee; but rather they will guard thee, and help thee more mightily in thy victory over the devil, while expecting also the incorruptible and everlasting crown of the victory.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: Who openeth the chambers of the womb?

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: At the end of the same section, after the words “Wise Creator,” this is found: “Glorify Him in His unsearchable works, and concerning Him whom thou art not capable of knowing, inquire not curiously what His essence is.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: It is better for thee to keep silence, and in faith adore, according to the divine Word, than daringly to search after things which neither thou canst reach, nor Holy Scripture hath delivered to thee.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: These points my discourse has now treated at large, that thou mayest abhor those who blaspheme the wise and good Artificer, and rather mayest thyself also say, How wonderful are Thy works O Lord; in wisdom hast Thou made them all.

verses.book: The Catechetical Lectures of S. Cyril.
verses.text: To Thee be the glory, and power, and worship, with the Holy Spirit, now and ever, and throughout all ages.
